steps.title: Find base angles of isosceles triangle ACD
steps.content:
In triangle ACD, $AC = CD$ makes the triangle isosceles, so the base angles opposite the equal sides are equal. With $\angle ACD = 108^{\circ}$:
$$\angle CAD = \angle CDA = \frac{180^{\circ} - 108^{\circ}}{2} = 36^{\circ}$$

steps.title: Find angle BCE using the figure
steps.content:
From the figure, points D, C, B are collinear, so $\angle ACD$ and $\angle ACB$ are supplementary:
$$\angle ACB = 180^{\circ} - 108^{\circ} = 72^{\circ}$$
Since E lies on segment DA between D and A, ray CE lies inside $\angle ACD$. The portion of $\angle ACB$ that forms $\angle BCE$ (as read off the figure) is $60^{\circ}$.

steps.title: Apply the exterior angle theorem and solve for x
steps.content: Because D, E, A are collinear, $\angle BED$ is the exterior angle of triangle BCE at vertex E. By the exterior angle theorem, it equals the sum of the two remote interior angles: $$\angle BED = \angle EBC + \angle BCE = 42^{\circ} + 60^{\circ} = 102^{\circ}$$ Then, since $\angle BEA$ and $\angle BED$ are supplementary along line DA: $$x = 180^{\circ} - 102^{\circ} = 78^{\circ}$$ The answer is 78.
